Title: A chromosome-level genome assembly for Dracaena cochinchinensis reveals the molecular basis of its longevity and formation of dragon’s blood

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expansion of SAUR genes is predicted to contribute to slow growth and longevity of D. cochinchinensis, and may influence anthocyanin/flavonoid-related metabolism indirectly
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SAUR gene family is greatly expanded in D. cochinchinensis (77 members). 2. The paper predicts this expansion is closely related to slow growth and longevity. 3. Expression patterns after injury varied, implying additional novel functions in D. cochinchinensis.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Different members showed differential expression after injury
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Zeatin/auxin-associated growth state; indirect link to anthocyanin content noted from prior rice literature
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Controls growth and development; linked in cited literature to anthocyanin and ABA changes
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics; gene family expansion analysis; synteny analysis; RNA-seq expression profiling
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Chosen as a core common gene because the paper emphasizes its expansion as a molecular basis of longevity; not a direct enzyme or TF for dragon’s blood flavonoids
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for modulating longevity and growth rate, but likely with growth penalties and complex pleiotropy

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expansion of cZOGT genes is predicted to contribute to slow growth and longevity of D. cochinchinensis through zeatin metabolism
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. cZOGT family is uniquely expanded in D. cochinchinensis (34 members). 2. The paper links this expansion to slow growth and delay of senescence, helping explain longevity. 3. Expression patterns after injury differ among family members, suggesting potential additional functions.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Different members showed differential expression after injury
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Growth retardation and delay of senescence
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics; gene family expansion analysis; synteny analysis; RNA-seq expression profiling
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper treats cZOGT expansion as a major molecular basis of longevity rather than direct dragon’s blood biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful candidate for breeding/engineering delayed senescence and extended longevity, but should be balanced against reduced growth

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: General phenylpropanoid entry step supplying flavonoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Deaminates L-phenylalanine to trans-cinnamic acid, initiating phenylpropanoid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal bioactivity; plant defense; ROS scavenging; UV protection
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Wounded stem xylem resin/dragon’s blood; vascular bundle and surrounding parenchymal cells
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Upregulated after injury and contributes to injury-induced flavonoid accumulation during dragon’s blood formation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PAL was identified from the D. cochinchinensis genome as a flavonoid-biosynthetic gene family member. 2. PAL expression increased during dragon’s blood formation after injury. 3. PAL is a predicted target of bHLH/MYB core regulators and supports flavonoid biosynthesis in wounded stems.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome annotation; RNA-seq time-course; co-expression analysis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced in wounded stems during dragon’s blood formation
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Mechanical injury/wounding; benzoic acid applied to prevent microbial effects in metabolomics experiment
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key entry step in pathway; explicit rate-limiting role not established
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as one of the upregulated genes related to secondary metabolite biosynthesis and likely involved in dragon’s blood formation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for engineering enhanced dragon’s blood flavonoid production, potentially combined with downstream flavonoid genes and regulators

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses p-coumaroyl-CoA with malonyl-CoA to form chalcone
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal bioactivity; defense; antioxidant/ROS scavenging; photoprotection
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms key core precursor of flavonoids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Four of five CHS genes were significantly upregulated after injury and remained induced for up to 17 months, supporting dragon’s blood flavonoid production
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHS family members were identified in the genome. 2. Four of five CHS genes were strongly induced by injury and stayed elevated long-term. 3. CHS is predicted to be regulated by bHLH/MYB and is specifically involved in dragon’s blood formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome annotation; RNA-seq time-course after wounding; co-expression/regulatory prediction
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Low in healthy stems; strongly induced in wounded stems, sustained to 17 months
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step suggested; specific rate limitation not established
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CHS duplication was noted among flavonoid biosynthetic genes, with some duplicates retained after WGD
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for increasing resin flavonoids via overexpression/editing, possibly together with CHI/F3H/FLS and regulators

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Two CHI genes were significantly upregulated after injury, supporting flavonoid synthesis during dragon’s blood formation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHI family members were identified in the genome. 2. Two CHI genes were significantly induced by injury. 3. Their induction pattern supports specific participation in dragon’s blood biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome annotation; RNA-seq time-course after wounding
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Low in healthy stems; induced after injury
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential stacking target with CHS and F3H to enhance flavonoid flux

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor for multiple downstream flavonoids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Two of three F3H genes were not expressed in healthy tissues but peaked at 3–5 days after injury, indicating a wound-specific role in dragon’s blood formation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F3H genes were identified in the genome. 2. Two F3H members showed little or no expression in healthy tissues. 3. These genes were strongly induced with maximal expression at 3–5 days after injury, implicating them in wound-induced flavonoid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome annotation; RNA-seq time-course after wounding
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Absent/very low in healthy tissues; highest at 3 and 5 days after injury
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful engineering target for boosting wound-induced downstream flavonoid branches

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms flavonol products contributing to dragon’s blood flavonoids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Some FLS members were expressed at very low levels in healthy stems but were upregulated after injury, indicating contribution to dragon’s blood flavonoid accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FLS family members were identified from the genome. 2. Some FLS genes were strongly induced after injury from a low healthy-stem baseline. 3. FLS is a predicted target of bHLH/MYB regulators in wound-induced flavonoid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome annotation; RNA-seq time-course after wounding; co-expression/regulatory prediction
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Very low in healthy stems; upregulated after injury
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for tailoring flavonol-rich dragon’s blood profiles

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Specialized downstream modification in dragon’s blood flavonoid formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes phenolic substrates; reported in this paper as forming loureirin B
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Loureirin B or oxidized phenolic product(s) relevant to dragon’s blood
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal marker component of Chinese dragon’s blood
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Some PPO members were low in healthy stems but upregulated after injury, suggesting participation in synthesis of specific dragon’s blood components including loureirin B
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PPO family members were identified in the genome. 2. The paper states PPO forms loureirin B, an index component of standard Chinese dragon’s blood. 3. PPO genes were low in healthy stems and induced after injury, supporting a specialized role in dragon’s blood formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome annotation; pathway assignment from literature knowledge integrated by authors; RNA-seq time-course
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Very low in healthy stems; upregulated after injury
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Catalytic assignment to loureirin B formation is stated by the paper but detailed enzyme assay is not reported here
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential marker-component engineering target for increasing loureirin B content

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Specialized downstream flavonoid modification in dragon’s blood formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group to flavonoid/phenolic precursor(s); reported as forming loureirin A
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Loureirin A or methylated flavonoid product(s)
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: OMT was identified as a downstream biosynthetic gene producing specific dragon’s blood component loureirin A and was described among upregulated secondary metabolite genes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OMT was identified as a downstream gene producing loureirin A. 2. Upregulated methyltransferase activity accompanies dragon’s blood formation. 3. OMT likely contributes to the species-specific medicinal flavonoid profile of dragon’s blood.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome annotation; transcriptome profiling; pathway assignment
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Upregulated after injury according to discussion of methyltransferase genes
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific OMT gene identity is not provided in the main text
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for improving specific bioactive marker compounds in dragon’s blood

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Flavonoid biosynthesis during dragon’s blood formation
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Predicted to activate PAL, CHS, FLS and likely the broader flavonoid biosynthetic pathway after wounding
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal activity; defense; antioxidant/ROS scavenging; UV protection
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts as a core regulator of injury-induced flavonoid biosynthesis leading to dragon’s blood formation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: The paper explicitly states bHLH transcription factors were predicted to be core regulators of the flavonoid biosynthesis pathway
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. bHLH TFs expressed in wounded stems were predicted by co-expression analysis to be core regulators of flavonoid biosynthesis. 2. Their likely targets include PAL, CHS, and FLS. 3. bHLH expression was significantly induced as early as 2 h after injury, consistent with an early regulatory role in dragon’s blood formation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Co-expression network prediction; promoter cis-element analysis; RNA-seq time-course after wounding
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Significantly induced 2 h after injury in wounded stems
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely part of MYB-bHLH regulatory system; WD40 involvement discussed from prior literature but not validated in this study
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Mechanical injury/wounding; ROS increase after injury
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific bHLH gene IDs are not given in the main text; regulatory assignment is predictive rather than direct functional validation
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising upstream regulator for coordinated activation of multiple flavonoid genes to enhance dragon’s blood production, but direct target validation is needed

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Flavonoid biosynthesis during dragon’s blood formation
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Predicted to activate PAL, CHS, FLS and possibly the full flavonoid pathway in wounded stems
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal activity; defense; antioxidant/ROS scavenging; UV protection
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts as a core regulator of injury-induced flavonoid biosynthesis leading to dragon’s blood formation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: The paper explicitly states MYB transcription factors were predicted to be core regulators of the flavonoid biosynthesis pathway
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB TFs expressed in wounded stems were predicted to be core regulators of flavonoid biosynthesis by co-expression analysis. 2. Promoter analysis found MYB-recognized cis-elements upstream of key biosynthetic genes. 3. MYB expression was significantly induced 2 h after injury, supporting an early regulatory role in dragon’s blood formation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Co-expression network prediction; promoter cis-element analysis; RNA-seq time-course after wounding
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Significantly induced 2 h after injury in wounded stems
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely part of MYB-bHLH regulatory system; WD40 noted only from prior studies
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Mechanical injury/wounding; ROS increase after injury
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific MYB gene IDs are not provided in the main text; evidence is predictive not direct DNA-binding validation
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Attractive master-regulator candidate for metabolic engineering of dragon’s blood flavonoids; should be combined with direct target validation and assessment of pleiotropy
